text: The surgeon begins the procedure by performing sharp dissection to separate dense intra-abdominal adhesions, using precise cutting techniques to navigate through the fibrous tissue.

The surgeon dissects the mesocolon, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues using surgical instruments.

Pus visibly flows out from the pelvic abscess, which is then aspirated using a surgical instrument.
Pus visibly flows out from the pelvic abscess, and the surgeon aspirates the purulent material using a suction device.
The surgeon dissects the left colic and sigmoidal arteries, applying purple hemoclips to secure these vessels.
The surgeon dissects the left colic and sigmoidal arteries and applies purple hemoclips to secure these vessels.
The surgeon ligates the left colic and sigmoidal arteries separately due to their large diameters, as the available hemoclips are not compatible with the current artery sizes.
The surgeon ligates the large-diameter arteries separately due to the unavailability of compatible hemoclips for their size.
The surgeon uses an energy device to divide the vessels, completing the dissection of the left colic and sigmoidal arteries.
The surgeon uses an energy device to divide the vessels, following the application of hemoclips on the left colic and sigmoidal arteries.
The surgeon carefully divides Told's fascia, revealing underlying anatomical structures as part of the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully divides Toldt's fascia, separating it from the underlying structures.
The surgical field shows the cleared pelvic area following the drainage of the abscess, with no visible pus remaining.

The surgeon prepares the rectosigmoid colon for resection, ensuring clear visualization and access to the targeted area.
The surgeon prepares the rectosigmoid colon for resection, ensuring clear delineation of the targeted segment for removal.
The surgeon completes the distal resection using a GIA stapler, creating a six-centimeter transection line across the prepared rectosigmoid cone.
The surgeon completes the distal resection using a GIA stapler, creating a staple line approximately six centimeters in length.
The surgeon performs a pharyngeal incision and extracts the specimen from the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon identifies the proximal border for resection at the middle of the descending colon, marking the area for surgical intervention.

The surgeon places an envelope circular stapler at the proximal end of the colon and reintroduces the colon into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon performs an end-to-end brictocolic anastomosis using a 31mm circular stapler, connecting the resected ends of the colon.
The surgeon performs an end-to-end brictocolic anastomosis using a 31mm circular stapler, joining the proximal and distal ends of the colon.
The surgeon performs an air leak test on the anastomosis site to visually assess its integrity, ensuring there are no leaks in the newly formed connection between the colon segments.
The surgeon performs an air leak test to evaluate the integrity of the end-to-end brictocolic anastomosis.
